qa_pairs.question: What modifications are necessary in Apache's `default-ssl.conf` file to configure it to use a newly created self-signed SSL certificate?
qa_pairs.answer: To configure Apache to use a self-signed SSL certificate, you must edit the `/etc/apache2/sites-available/default-ssl.conf` file. Specifically, change the `SSLCertificateFile` and `SSLCertificateKeyFile` lines to point to `/etc/ssl/certs/server.crt` and `/etc/ssl/private/server.key` respectively. Additionally, ensure both `default-ssl.conf` and `000-default.conf` have the same `DocumentRoot` variables.

qa_pairs.question: After configuring a self-signed SSL certificate on an Apache web server, what steps should be taken to tighten security, including forcing HTTP traffic to HTTPS?
qa_pairs.answer: To tighten security, first edit `/etc/apache2/sites-available/default-ssl.conf` and add `ServerName XXX-XXX-XXX-XXX.cloud.computecanada.ca`, `SSLProtocol all -SSLv2 -SSLv3`, `SSLCipherSuite HIGH:MEDIUM:!aNULL:!MD5:!SEED:!IDEA:!RC4`, and `SSLHonorCipherOrder on` inside the `<VirtualHost>` tag, replacing `XXX-XXX-XXX-XXX` with your VM's public IP. Secondly, edit `/etc/apache2/sites-available/000-default.conf` and add the line `Redirect permanent / https://XXX-XXX-XXX-XXX.cloud.computecanada.ca` inside its `<VirtualHost>` tag to force all HTTP traffic to HTTPS.

qa_pairs.question: Once Apache is configured with a self-signed SSL certificate, how do you enable the SSL-enabled website and apply the changes?
qa_pairs.answer: To enable the SSL-enabled website and apply the configuration changes, you must execute the commands `sudo a2ensite default-ssl.conf` followed by `sudo service apache2 restart`.
